summary:
This PR fixes a bug in `iter_lines()` where a `
` pair split across two chunks generates a spurious extra newline. The fix introduces a `last_chunk_ends_with_cr` flag and sentinel variables to handle the cross-chunk CRLF case. However, a critical correctness bug remains: when a chunk is exactly `
` and gets consumed via `continue`, the flag is never reset to `False`, causing subsequent `
`-starting chunks to be incorrectly stripped. Several test coverage gaps and style issues are also present.

findings.title: `last_chunk_ends_with_cr` not reset before `continue`, causing blank lines to be silently dropped
findings.detail:
When `last_chunk_ends_with_cr` is `True` and the current chunk is exactly `b'\n'` (or `u'\n'`), the leading `\n` is stripped (line 813), leaving an empty chunk. The code then hits `continue` at line 816 **without** resetting `last_chunk_ends_with_cr` to `False` (the reset assignment on line 819 is skipped entirely). On the very next non-empty chunk, the flag is still `True`, so if that chunk also starts with `\n` its leading `\n` will be incorrectly stripped, merging what is actually a genuine blank line into the preceding content.

Reproducer:
```python
chunks = [b'a\r', b'\n', b'\nfoo']
# Expected (matches b'a\r\n\nfoo'.splitlines()): [b'a', b'', b'foo']
# Actual: [b'a', b'foo']  # blank line silently lost
```

Fix: reset the flag immediately before (or as part of) the `continue`:
```python
if not chunk:
    last_chunk_ends_with_cr = False
    continue
```

The existing parametrized test suite does not include a case that exercises this sub-branch (e.g. `[b'a\r', b'\n', b'\nfoo']` or `[b'line\r', b'\n']`). Tests covering this exact path should be added.

findings.title: CRLF split across `pending`+chunk boundary may not be correctly collapsed
findings.detail: The `pending` mechanism prepends the tail of the previous chunk to the current chunk (before the CR/LF stitching logic). If `pending` itself ends with `\r` and the new incoming chunk starts with `\n`, after prepending, `chunk` no longer starts with `\n` at index 0 — it starts with whatever `pending` starts with. The guard `chunk.startswith(line_feed)` therefore evaluates the already-merged `pending + chunk` and will fail to detect the cross-boundary CRLF, potentially emitting an extra blank line. While not a direct security vulnerability, this can cause protocol-level confusion in security-sensitive line-oriented protocols (HTTP chunked trailers, SSE streams, etc.).

findings.title: Redundant intermediate variables `carriage_return` / `line_feed`
findings.detail: Both variables are each used exactly once (lines 809 and 819). The existing code style in this file inlines simple expressions rather than hoisting them into named variables. Inlining them at the call sites (or at least dropping the explicit `u''` prefix, which is the Python 3 default) would be more consistent with the surrounding style.

findings.title: Type mismatch risk: `decode_unicode=None` vs `decode_unicode=False` sentinel selection
findings.detail: The sentinel variables `carriage_return` and `line_feed` are chosen by branching on the truthiness of `decode_unicode`, which defaults to `None` in `iter_lines`. Both `None` and `False` are falsy and correctly select the bytes sentinels. However, `decode_unicode` is forwarded verbatim to `iter_content()`, which defaults the parameter to `False` rather than `None`. If `iter_content` ever treats `None` differently from `False` (or changes its default), the sentinel type computed here (bytes) could mismatch the chunk type actually produced (str), silently corrupting `startswith`/`endswith` comparisons on lines 809 and 819 and causing silent data corruption for stream consumers.

findings.title: No test with explicit `decode_unicode=False` for the CRLF-split fix
findings.detail: The new `carriage_return`/`line_feed` sentinels are selected based on the truthiness of `decode_unicode`. All tests either pass `decode_unicode=True` or omit it (leaving it as `None`). There is no test that explicitly passes `decode_unicode=False`. While `None` and `False` are currently both falsy and select the same bytes path, an explicit test would guard against future regressions that distinguish `None` from `False`.

findings.title: Missing space after comma in parametrize tuple
findings.detail:
Line 1371 has `[b'a
',b'b
']` — a space is missing after the comma. The surrounding parametrize entries and the existing test-file style use a space after every comma. It should read `[b'a
', b'b
', ...]`.

findings.title: `mock_iter_content` helper duplicated across two test methods
findings.detail: An identical `mock_iter_content` helper function is defined in both `test_response_lines` (line 1322) and `test_response_lines_parametrized` (line 1384). This violates the DRY principle visible in the rest of the test class. Extract it to a module-level helper or a shared fixture.

findings.title: Inconsistent comment style and verbosity in new code block
findings.detail:
Two issues with the comments in the new block:
1. The comment above `if not chunk:` (line 814) is verbose for a simple empty-check guard. Consider shortening to `# chunk may now be empty` to match the terse register of surrounding comments.
2. `# check if the current chunk ends with '\r'` (line 818) is lowercase, while the comment on lines 810–812 (`# The last chunk ends with '\r'...`) uses sentence case. All inline comments in this block should use the same capitalisation convention, consistent with the rest of the file.

findings.title: Long assertion line exceeds apparent line-length convention
findings.detail: `assert list(r.iter_lines(decode_unicode=True, delimiter=u'\r\n')) == unicode_mock_data.split(u'\r\n')` is a single long line. The surrounding test file breaks long expressions across multiple lines for readability; consider doing the same here.

findings.title: `last_chunk_ends_with_cr` not reset to `False` before `continue` when chunk becomes empty
findings.detail:
When `last_chunk_ends_with_cr` is `True` and the current chunk is exactly `b'\n'` (or `u'\n'`), the code strips the `\n` (line 813), leaving an empty chunk. It then hits `continue` at line 816 **without** resetting `last_chunk_ends_with_cr` to `False`.

On the very next non-empty chunk, `last_chunk_ends_with_cr` is still `True`, so if that chunk also starts with `\n` the code will incorrectly strip its leading `\n`, merging what is actually a genuine blank line into the preceding content.

Example reproducer:
  chunks = [b'a\r', b'\n', b'\nfoo']
  Expected lines (same as b'a\r\n\nfoo'.splitlines()): [b'a', b'', b'foo']
  Actual lines with this bug: [b'a', b'foo']  (the blank line is lost)

Fix: reset `last_chunk_ends_with_cr = False` immediately before (or as part of) the `continue` at line 816:

```python
    if not chunk:
        last_chunk_ends_with_cr = False
        continue
```

findings.title: decode_unicode=None treated as falsy, allowing type confusion between bytes and str modes
findings.detail: The expressions `u'\r' if decode_unicode else b'\r'` and `u'\n' if decode_unicode else b'\n'` branch on the truthiness of `decode_unicode`. The parameter's default value is `None`, and callers who explicitly pass `decode_unicode=False` will correctly get bytes. However, `decode_unicode=None` and `decode_unicode=False` are both falsy and produce bytes sentinels, which is correct here. The concern is that the same `decode_unicode` value is forwarded verbatim to `iter_content()` (line 786), where `iter_content` defaults the parameter to `False`, not `None`. If `iter_content` ever treats `None` as truthy (or changes its default), the sentinel type computed here (bytes) would mismatch the chunk type actually produced (str), silently corrupting the `startswith`/`endswith` comparisons on lines 809 and 819. No immediate exploit, but the mismatch between `None` defaults on `iter_lines` vs `False` defaults on `iter_content` is a subtle correctness/integrity issue that could cause silent data corruption for stream consumers who rely on the sanitised output.

findings.title: CR/LF stripping logic can be bypassed when 'pending' data crosses a chunk boundary
findings.detail:
The `last_chunk_ends_with_cr` flag is set based on the *modified* chunk (line 819, after the possible `chunk = chunk[1:]` strip). However, the `pending` mechanism prepends the tail of the previous chunk to the *current* chunk (lines 795-797) *before* the CR/LF stitching logic is applied. This means: if a prior chunk ends with `b'text'`, its `` is held in `pending` (not yet emitted), and in the next iteration, `chunk = pending + chunk`. If that combined chunk now ends with `` and the *following* chunk starts with `
`, `last_chunk_ends_with_cr` will correctly be `True`. However if `pending` itself ends with `` and the new chunk (before prepending) starts with `
`, the prepend causes `chunk = pending + b'
...'` which does NOT start with `
` at index 0 of the raw incoming chunk — the check at line 809 evaluates the already-merged chunk, so the `
` is now at `len(pending)`, not at position 0. The guard `chunk.startswith(line_feed)` will fail because `chunk` (after prepend) starts with whatever `pending` starts with. This means CRLF split across a pending+chunk boundary is not correctly collapsed, potentially leaking an extra empty line to the caller. While not a direct security vulnerability, if iter_lines is used to parse security-sensitive line-oriented protocols (HTTP chunked trailers, SSE event streams, etc.), extra blank lines can cause protocol-level confusion or bypass line-count-based rate limits.

findings.title: Redundant intermediate variables `carriage_return` / `line_feed`
findings.detail:
The two variables are each used exactly once (lines 809 and 819). The existing code style in this file inlines simple expressions rather than hoisting them into named variables. Inlining them — e.g. `chunk.startswith(b'
' if not decode_unicode else u'
')` or, more readably, just computing them inline at the call sites — would match the surrounding style. Alternatively, if kept as variables, the names are reasonable but the `u''` / `b''` distinction can simply be expressed as `b'
'` vs `'
'` without the explicit `u` prefix (Python 3 default), consistent with the rest of the file.

findings.title: Comment on same line as code without a blank line before the inline comment
findings.detail: The inline comment `# it's possible that after stripping the '\n' then chunk becomes empty` is placed above `if not chunk:` but the indentation and comment style doesn't match the project style of terse inline comments. The surrounding comments in the same block use the style of a comment on its own line before the relevant code, which this does follow — but the comment is verbose for a simple empty-check guard. Consider shortening it to `# chunk may now be empty` to match the terse register of surrounding comments like `# check if the current chunk ends with '\r'`.

findings.title: Inconsistent comment style: lowercase vs. sentence-cased comments
findings.detail: `# check if the current chunk ends with '\r'` is lowercase while `# The last chunk ends with '\r', so the '\n' at chunk[0]...` (line 810–812) uses sentence case. The file's existing inline comments use sentence case (see e.g. line 16–17 of the style sample). Both inline comments in this block should use the same capitalisation convention.

findings.title: Missing spaces after commas in parametrize tuples
findings.detail:
Lines 1371 and 1374 have `[b'a
',b'b
']` and `[b'a
b', b'', b'c']` — the first is missing a space after the comma. The surrounding parametrize entries and the existing test-file style use a space after every comma. Line 1371 should read `[b'a
', b'b
', ...]`.

findings.title: `mock_iter_content` helper duplicated in two test methods
findings.detail: An identical `mock_iter_content` helper function is defined in both `test_response_lines` (line 1322) and `test_response_lines_parametrized` (line 1384). The existing test file style uses lambdas for small one-liners, but since both methods now need the same two-branch function it could be extracted to a module-level helper or at least a shared fixture, matching the DRY approach visible in the rest of the test class. As-is it is a readability/maintenance concern.

findings.title: Line exceeds apparent line-length convention
findings.detail: `assert list(r.iter_lines(decode_unicode=True, delimiter=u'\r\n')) == unicode_mock_data.split(u'\r\n')` is a single long line. The surrounding test file breaks long expressions across lines. Consider splitting it for consistency.

findings.title: No test for chunk becoming empty after stripping leading '\n' (the `continue` branch)
findings.detail:
The code at line 815-816 handles the case where after stripping the leading '
' from a chunk (because last_chunk_ends_with_cr was True), the chunk becomes empty and the loop does `continue`. None of the parametrized test cases in `test_response_lines_parametrized` or `test_response_lines` exercise this specific sub-branch: a split where one chunk is exactly '' and the next is exactly '
' (so that after stripping '
' the chunk is empty and `continue` is hit). The closest case [b'a', b'
b'] still has content after the '
'. A case like [b'', b'
'] or [b'line', b'
'] is needed to cover the `continue` on line 816.

findings.title: No test for `last_chunk_ends_with_cr` reset when chunk is consumed via `continue`
findings.detail:
When a chunk consisting solely of '
' is consumed via `continue` (line 816), `last_chunk_ends_with_cr` is never updated (the assignment on line 819 is skipped). If the following chunk also starts with '
', it would not be incorrectly skipped because `last_chunk_ends_with_cr` was reset to False by the previous non-empty chunk. However, the interaction between the `continue` path and the state of `last_chunk_ends_with_cr` for subsequent chunks is not tested. A sequence like [b'a', b'
', b'
b'] would cover this path.

findings.title: No test with `decode_unicode=False` (explicit False vs. None/falsy) for the CRLF-split fix
findings.detail: The new variables `carriage_return` and `line_feed` are set based on `decode_unicode`. All tests pass either `decode_unicode=True` or omit it (None). There is no test that explicitly passes `decode_unicode=False`. While None and False are both falsy and would both select the bytes path, an explicit test would guard against future changes distinguishing None from False.

findings.title: `last_chunk_ends_with_cr` not reset before `continue`, causing blank lines to be silently dropped
findings.detail:
When `last_chunk_ends_with_cr` is `True` and the current chunk is exactly `b'\n'` (or `u'\n'`), the leading `\n` is stripped (line 813), leaving an empty chunk. # Expected (matches b'a\r\n\nfoo'.splitlines()): [b'a', b'', b'foo']

findings.detail:
Line 1371 has `[b'a
',b'b
']` — a space is missing after the comma. The surrounding parametrize entries and the existing test-file style use a space after every comma. It should read `[b'a
', b'b
', ...]`.

findings.detail: An identical `mock_iter_content` helper function is defined in both `test_response_lines` (line 1322) and `test_response_lines_parametrized` (line 1384). This violates the DRY principle visible in the rest of the test class. Extract it to a module-level helper or a shared fixture.

Two issues with the comments in the new block:
1. The comment above `if not chunk:` (line 814) is verbose for a simple empty-check guard. Consider shortening to `# chunk may now be empty` to match the terse register of surrounding comments.
2. `# check if the current chunk ends with '\r'` (line 818) is lowercase, while the comment on lines 810–812 (`# The last chunk ends with '\r'...`) uses sentence case. All inline comments in this block should use the same capitalisation convention, consistent with the rest of the file.

findings.title: Long assertion line exceeds apparent line-length convention
findings.detail: `assert list(r.iter_lines(decode_unicode=True, delimiter=u'\r\n')) == unicode_mock_data.split(u'\r\n')` is a single long line. The surrounding test file breaks long expressions across multiple lines for readability; consider doing the same here.
